sourceText: When employing people, one should not only take their strengths but also discard their weaknesses.
Emphasizes the importance of considering both the strengths and weaknesses of individuals when appointing them to suitable positions.
Adversity tests courage, and success tests wisdom.
Signifies that hardship tests courage, while prosperity tests wise judgment, highlighting the need to maintain the right attitude in any situation.
Political persecution, suppression due to Catholic faith, exile
Continued academic research amidst hardship, wrote seminal works, and established reformist ideas for solving real-world problems
Learning is like a river that never stops; if its flow ceases, it becomes stagnant.
Emphasizes that knowledge and wisdom develop only through a continuous attitude of learning and exploration.
Without a heart that loves the people, one cannot govern a nation.
Signifies that rulers must prioritize the people and govern with a heart that cares for them.
Reading ten thousand books is useless if you do not put them into practice.
States that no matter how much knowledge one accumulates, it is worthless if not applied and practiced in real life.

Mencius faced frustration as his attempts to realize his ideal governance in the chaotic Warring States period were met with the reality of political limitations. He also encountered criticism and misunderstanding from those who did not grasp his philosophy.

Despite setbacks, Mencius upheld his convictions. He focused on transmitting his philosophy to future generations by teaching his disciples and authoring the 'Mencius'. Through these efforts, his thought has transcended time and continues to have a profound impact today.
